summary: Steve Yegge recounts his journey from abandoning Android development due to painful Java APIs to falling unexpectedly in love with Kotlin. After a decade of searching for a Java replacement, he discovered Kotlin while attempting to port his game Wyvern to Android. Within weeks of experimenting, he was so impressed that he began rewriting his entire 20-year-old game server in Kotlin. He argues that Kotlin's killer app is Android itself—the platform's frustrating APIs created demand for a better language, and Kotlin delivers by being fully Java-interoperable while making code dramatically more pleasant to write.
key_points: Kotlin succeeded where other JVM languages failed because it offers seamless Java interoperability—you can mix Kotlin and Java in the same project and migrate incrementally
Android's notoriously bad APIs (Fragments, lifecycle management) created the perfect opportunity for Kotlin to thrive as a 'killer app' platform
JetBrains' background as IDE makers gave Kotlin unprecedented tooling support from day one, making it exceptionally easy to learn and use
Kotlin is practical and accessible to working programmers—it's not 'weird' like Scala or Clojure, but rather feels like a natural evolution from Java
The language restored Yegge's enjoyment of programming after years of Java-induced jadedness, making him feel like a hobbyist again
money_quotes: If the language you're using happens to be Java, then you've no doubt realized that by the time Java becomes a really good language, you'll be dead. Loooong dead.
I'm so jaded that I didn't think it was possible to love a language ever again, but Kotlin is just gorgeous.
Java 8 lets you say interesting things, but you have to do it with a mouthful of sand.
I just want street food, you know? Scala is nice but it's just too fancy for me, all frog legs and calf brains and truffled snails.

It only took maybe 3 days to learn Kotlin well enough to start busting out code, fully aware that I didn't know what the hell I was doing, but knowing the language and IDE were doing a great job of keeping me out of trouble anyway.
We hadn't fully grasped that programming had become non-fun until we tried Kotlin. It takes you back to when you were first learning programming and everything seemed achievable.
Kotlin manages to help you route around just about all of Android's Red Lights, and turns the experience into something that on the whole I now find superior to iOS development.
key_insight: A new programming language can only succeed if it has a 'killer app' platform that makes developers willing to learn it, and Kotlin found that killer app in Android's frustratingly verbose Java APIs.
